mappings.rationale: Directly addresses social differentiation, stratification, class systems, status, and social mobility (outline A and B).
Examines caste systems, age and sex differentiation, and comparative approaches to social stratification across societies (outline A.2 and B.1).
Directly addresses upper class, elites, feudalism, and hereditary status-based stratification (outline B.1 and B.3).
Marxist theories of social class, class conflict, and the idea of a classless society are foundational to outline B.2 and B.5.
Engels' theories of the working class, middle class, and historical materialism provide a framework for understanding class structure (outline B.2 and B.3).
Capitalist economic relations create class stratification through ownership and production, central to outline A.3.a and B.3.
Racial differentiation as a factor producing social and economic inequality is central to outline A.2.
Sex differentiation as a factor in social stratification and challenges to gender-based hierarchies are explored in relation to outline A.2.
The historical role of gender and its intersectionality with class and race illuminates patterns of social stratification (outline A.2).
The Hindu caste system stands as a major example of social stratification and its relation to class (outline B.1).
Slavery as an extreme form of social stratification based on race shaped mobility and inequality (outline A.2 and B.1).
The communist pursuit of a classless society represents an alternative approach to eliminating stratification (outline B.5).
Socialist theories of class relationships and visions of social equality are central to outline B.2 and B.5.
Democratic governance relates to class, status, and power as forms of stratification (outline A.3.b).
Tocqueville's analysis of democratic equality, social mobility, and class structure informs outline B.4 and B.5.
Ideological frameworks justify or challenge systems of social stratification (outline B.2).
Economic inequality at the bottom of the stratification system, including income distribution, is central to outline A.3.a and B.3.
Economic development has shaped wealth distribution and class formation across societies (outline A.3.a).
Psychological effects of social differentiation on individuals -- including prejudice and group identity -- connect to outline A.3.c.
Challenges to racial stratification and the pursuit of social mobility and equality are central to outline B.4.
Identity intersects with class, status, and power in stratified societies (outline A.3.c).
Populist challenges to elite power and class-based stratification are relevant to outline A.3.b and B.2.
Caste as a system of social stratification and positive discrimination as a form of social mobility illuminate outline B.1 and B.4.
Revolutions challenge class systems and attempt to create new forms of social equality (outline B.5).
Industrialization created new forms of class stratification including the working and middle classes (outline A.3.a and B.3).
